overall: The architecture covers the main audit loop and most trust-critical invariants, but it is not clean enough to hand directly to api-contracts/specs/implementation-plan without rework. The main risks are contradictory ownership boundaries for capture interfaces and .surface writes, plus incomplete data flows for several Must-have input/interface paths.

findings.location: §1 Architectural style, §5 Module structure, §6 Extension points
findings.finding: The document says every plugin interface, including CaptureBackend, is defined in core/src/interfaces, but ADR-004's accepted decision says the single capture interface is defined in packages/capture. This is an ADR/prose conflict and also changes the dependency shape for the capture package.
findings.suggestion: Either move the capture interface ownership back to @surface/capture in §1/§5/§6, or explicitly amend/supersede ADR-004 before downstream specs rely on core owning CaptureBackend.

findings.location: §4.1 Audit flow, §4.3 Export flow, §7 State management
findings.finding: The architecture states .surface is locked/atomic state, but the flows make Reporters and export write local artifacts directly, and Capture implicitly persists artifacts. The Project State domain says Project State is the only context that writes .surface, so implementers could bypass the lock or split writes across packages.
findings.suggestion: Introduce an explicit StateStore/ArtifactStore boundary in core/state: capture, reporters, and exporters return bytes/metadata or write intents; only the state layer writes .surface under the lock and records artifact paths/events.

findings.finding: The only primary audit flow is 'Audit a route', but release-gate Must requirements include static/source inputs, screenshots, DOM snapshots, context inputs, personas/tasks/product docs, and Scaffold artifacts. These are not shown entering Capture/Evaluation/Project State, so non-live audit behavior and degradation semantics are underspecified.
findings.suggestion: Add a second audit flow for non-live/context-heavy targets showing Target construction for source/component/screenshot/dom, context ingestion into Persona/TaskDefinition/SurfaceConfig, what artifacts are unavailable, and how skipped measured checks are reported.

findings.finding: The re-audit flow lets any prior TrackedFinding become resolved when the validation check passes, but the Closed Loop domain requires gatedForHuman findings to require a Verdict or human-confirmed validation before resolution by agent action. As written, risky judged/brand/copy/critical-flow issues can be silently closed by automation.
findings.suggestion: Add a gatedForHuman branch to §4.2: agent revalidation may mark technical checks passing, but status cannot transition to resolved until a Verdict or human-confirmed validation event is recorded.

findings.finding: Reporter is modeled as render(findings, backlog): Report, but §4.3 uses Reporters.export(github) for side-effectful tracker creation, and ADR-016/domain Reporting distinguish local renderers, gate evaluation, and IssueExport. The single Reporter interface is too narrow and conflates pure rendering with external integration.
findings.suggestion: Split extension points into ReportRenderer, GateEvaluator, and IssueExporter. Make IssueExporter consume a persisted local artifact/backlog reference and produce IssueExport synced/unsynced state; keep renderers pure and local-first.

findings.finding: Leaf packages are told to implement core/src/interfaces, which invites deep imports and contradicts project-structure's published-entry-point-only rule. This is a dependency hygiene and circular-dependency risk once packages start importing concrete core internals.
findings.suggestion: Specify package export paths such as @surface/core/interfaces and @surface/core/schema, ban @surface/core/src/* imports in lint, and state that interfaces are imported only through published package exports.

findings.finding: The 'Cannot be extended without an ADR' set omits several accepted architectural constraints: adding a database/persistence backend, adding REST or a remote MCP listener, default telemetry, model proxying/bundled inference, and default capture exfiltration. §10 even mentions SQLite as an escape hatch without saying it requires an ADR.
findings.suggestion: Expand the no-extension-without-ADR list to include persistence backend changes, any new public/network API surface, telemetry, model proxy/billing, and any default-on data egress. Tie the SQLite escape hatch explicitly to a future ADR.

findings.finding: §7 says older runHistory rolls to history.log outside the locked aggregate, while §7/§10 also present one lock for .surface writes. 'Outside the aggregate' can be read as outside lock protection, which would violate PS-I1 for writes under .surface.
findings.suggestion: Clarify that history.log is outside state.json's rewritten aggregate but still written under the same .surface StateLock, or move it outside .surface if it is intentionally not protected.

findings.finding: The auth flow is CLI-only, but FR-CAP-8 requires both CLI and MCP to accept injected session state. The architecture otherwise treats CLI/MCP as parallel adapters, so this omission can lead to an MCP contract gap.
findings.suggestion: Change the flow entry to CLI/MCP and state how auth-state is passed in MCP tool input, validated, redacted from logs, and mapped to structured MCP errors.
